title: 5 Iconic Movies That Almost Got Banned Before They Even Hit Theaters
dek: Before they became classics, these five films had to fight the MPAA tooth and nail — cutting scenes, losing appeals, and sometimes just saying 'screw it' and going unrated.

items.heading: Requiem For A Dream Lost Its Appeal — And Went Out Unrated Anyway
items.text: Darren Aronofsky's addiction nightmare got slapped with an NC-17 almost entirely over its final montage, especially the scene where Marion performs for a room of men. Aronofsky appealed, arguing that trimming even a frame would gut the film's message, but the appeal was denied and Artisan released it unrated in theaters anyway, with a softer R-rated cut following later for video.

images.caption: Released unrated after the MPAA denied Aronofsky's appeal
Aronofsky refused to cut the film's climax

items.heading: Team America Had To Resubmit Its Puppet Sex Scene NINE Times
items.text: Trey Parker and Matt Stone's marionette sex scene between Gary and Lisa was originally a minute and a half long, and the MPAA kept slapping it with an NC-17 no matter how much they trimmed it. The scene had to be submitted nine separate times — with cuts to positions, urinating, and defecating puppets — before it finally scraped by with an R just days before release, and the extended cut later surfaced unrated on DVD.

images.caption: The puppet love scene that baffled the MPAA nine times over
Parker mocked the MPAA's objections to puppet 'positions'

items.heading: Boys Don't Cry Got Hit With NC-17 Over A Female Orgasm — Not The Rape Scene
items.text: Kimberly Peirce's Brandon Teena drama initially received an NC-17 largely because the MPAA felt a same-sex love scene — and the length of Lana's orgasm during it — was too explicit, even as they had no issue with the brutal rape and murder sequence. Peirce ultimately trimmed the love scene to secure an R rating so Fox Searchlight would distribute it, a decision she called out for its blatant double standard around sex versus violence.

images.caption: Trimmed for an R after the MPAA objected to a love scene, not the violence
Swank starred as Brandon Teena in the Oscar-winning drama

items.heading: Basic Instinct Got Resubmitted To The MPAA Eight Times Before It Squeaked By
items.text: Paul Verhoeven's erotic thriller was originally rated NC-17 for its explicit sex and violence, and Verhoeven ended up trimming roughly 35 to 40 seconds — softening the opening ice-pick murder and a rough sex scene — to land an R. Contractually obligated to deliver that R rating, he submitted the film to the board at least eight times before it passed, and the uncut version later surfaced unrated on home video while the theatrical cut became a massive box office hit.

images.caption: Cut by 35-40 seconds after multiple NC-17 rejections
Stone's breakout role sparked both controversy and box office gold

items.heading: American Psycho Nearly Lost Its R Rating Over 18 Seconds Of A Threesome
items.text: Mary Harron's satire hit an NC-17 wall over a three-way sex scene between Patrick Bateman and two escorts, even though the film's chainsaw murders sailed through without a single note. After Lions Gate appealed and lost, Harron trimmed about 18 seconds — mostly nudity and a suggestion of rear-entry sex — while keeping the scene's mirror-gazing centerpiece intact, and secured the R rating she needed for a wide release.

images.caption: Trimmed by 18 seconds to dodge an NC-17 over its threesome scene
Bale's Patrick Bateman survived the cuts — his violence never got flagged
